name: MinIO
version: Community repo archived; AIStor docs active
version_status: CONFIRMED strategic change / exact CE status requires human review
license: Historically AGPLv3 for community code; current AIStor materials emphasize commercial licensing
license_status: CONFIRMED strategic licensing shift / exact packaging requires human review
what: S3-compatible object storage previously common in self-hosted stacks for files, backups, and media.
solves: Internal object storage for uploads, documents, backups, artifacts, and application binary payloads.
users: Infrastructure teams that need S3-compatible storage under their own control.
fit: Technically useful, but strategically risky for new standardization because the public community repo appears archived and the official site/docs now center on commercial AIStor.
runtime: Distributed or single-node object storage service with S3 API compatibility, erasure coding, and policy controls.
components: Storage nodes, TLS, access policies, buckets, backup/replication design, and monitoring.
deploy: Historically container- or Kubernetes-friendly; current official guidance is centered on AIStor and its operator/helm-based patterns.
deps: Durable disks, network throughput, TLS, backup/replication design, S3-compatible client validation.
datastores: Object data on disk; metadata managed by the service.
auth: Access keys, policies, TLS, and admin controls.
apis: S3-compatible API, console/admin endpoints, replication and lifecycle features.
security: Object storage becomes a central data plane. Misconfigured buckets, replication, or lifecycle rules create serious exposure.
setup_path: Do not assume legacy MinIO community guidance still matches the 2026 reality. Revalidate whether Hylono wants MinIO specifically, an AIStor commercial commitment, or another object-storage path.
env: Root credentials, domain/bucket config, storage layout, TLS certs, and policy settings.
persistence: Object storage durability depends on storage design, replication, and tested backup/restore procedures.
operations: Capacity monitoring, lifecycle rule validation, bucket policy review, and strategic vendor/license review.
failure_points: Assuming community MinIO remains a stable default, underestimating bucket-policy risk, and building new dependencies before clarifying the product/licensing direction.
integration: Would be useful for Documenso/DocuSeal documents, Medusa assets, Listmonk media, backups, and possibly Next.js uploads—but only after the product/licensing direction is settled.
verify: Verify whether Hylono already uses MinIO, whether that deployment is supportable, and whether a different object storage platform is preferable for new rollout work.
risk: High strategic risk for new adoption because of the apparent community/archive/commercial shift.
gdpr: Object storage may contain signed documents, uploads, backups, and audit artifacts. EU hosting, encryption, retention, and access logging matter.
recommendation: DO NOT EXPAND UNTIL PRODUCT/LICENSE DIRECTION IS DECIDED
overlap: Can be replaced by managed S3-compatible storage or alternative self-hosted object stores.
codex_read_first: Read the official site/docs state and repository status before coding against MinIO assumptions.
codex_best_path: If object storage is needed immediately, verify the currently running environment first; otherwise keep the abstraction S3-compatible so the backend can be swapped.
codex_never_assume: Never assume legacy MinIO CE deployment advice is still the preferred path for a new 2026 deployment.

sources.why: Used to inspect public repo status and recent release history.
